title: Franciscan

infobox.value: [OFM](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") (1897) [OFM Conventual](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") (1209) [OFM Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") (1520) [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis") (1221)

(grey/gray friars) are a group of related organizations in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), founded by the Italian saint [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"). They include three independent [religious orders](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") for men (the [Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") being the largest contemporary male order), an order for [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun") known as the [Order of Saint Clare](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Clare "Order of Saint Clare"), and the [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis"), a [religious](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis#Third_Order_Regular "Third Order of Saint Francis") and [secular](/wiki/Secular_Franciscan_Order "Secular Franciscan Order") group open to male and female members.

or **greyfriars** because of their [habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit"). In Poland and Lithuania they are known as **[Bernardines](/wiki/Bernardines_(Franciscans) "Bernardines (Franciscans)")**, after [Bernardino of Siena](/wiki/Bernardino_of_Siena "Bernardino of Siena"), although the term elsewhere refers to [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians") instead.

are commonly called simply the **Franciscans**. This order is a [mendicant](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") [religious order](/wiki/Religious_order_(Catholic) "Religious order (Catholic)") of men, some of whom trace their origin to Francis of Assisi.[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10) Their official [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name is the *Ordo Fratrum Minorum*.[[11]](#cite_note-ceofm-11) Francis thus referred to his followers as "[Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli")", meaning "Little Brothers". Franciscan brothers are informally called **friars** or the **Minorites**.[[12]](#cite_note-cefran-12)

in English-speaking countries, consists of one branch of religious sisters. The order is called the Order of St. Clare (OSC). Prior to 1263 they were called "The Poor Ladies", "The Poor Enclosed Nuns", and "The Order of San Damiano".[[13]](#cite_note-13)
(TOR) live in religious communities under the traditional [religious vows](/wiki/Religious_vows "Religious vows"). They grew out of what became the Secular Franciscan Order.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Franciscan](/w/index.php?title=Franciscan&redirect=no "Franciscan"))

Group of religious orders within the Catholic Church connected with St. Francis of Assisi

"Minorites" redirects here; not to be confused with [Minorities](/wiki/Minorities "Minorities").

"Grey Friars" redirects here. For other uses, see [Greyfriars (disambiguation)](/wiki/Greyfriars_(disambiguation) "Greyfriars (disambiguation)").

Franciscans

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| A cross, Christ's arm and Saint Francis' arm, a universal symbol of the Franciscans[[1]](#cite_note-sbf-1) | |
| Formation | 24 February 1209; 817 years ago (1209-02-24) |
| Founder | [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") |
| Motto | *Pax et bonum* (Latin) *Peace and [all] good* |
| Parent organization | [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") |
| [Subsidiaries](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary") | [OFM](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") (1897) [OFM Conventual](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") (1209) [OFM Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") (1520) [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis") (1221) |

The **Franciscans** (grey/gray friars) are a group of related organizations in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), founded by the Italian saint [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"). They include three independent [religious orders](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") for men (the [Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") being the largest contemporary male order), an order for [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun") known as the [Order of Saint Clare](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Clare "Order of Saint Clare"), and the [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis"), a [religious](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis#Third_Order_Regular "Third Order of Saint Francis") and [secular](/wiki/Secular_Franciscan_Order "Secular Franciscan Order") group open to male and female members.

Franciscans adhere to the teachings and spiritual disciplines of the founder and of his main associates and followers, such as [Clare of Assisi](/wiki/Clare_of_Assisi "Clare of Assisi"), [Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua"), and [Elizabeth of Hungary](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Hungary "Elizabeth of Hungary"). Several smaller [Protestant Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan_spirituality_in_Protestantism "Franciscan spirituality in Protestantism") orders have been established since the late 19th century as well, particularly in the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") traditions.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Certain Franciscan communities are [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") in nature, having members who belong to several [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination").[[4]](#cite_note-Pitchford2006-4)

Francis began preaching around 1207 and traveled to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") to seek approval from [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III") in 1209 to form a religious order. The original [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis") approved by the pope did not allow ownership of property, requiring members of the order to beg for food while preaching. The austerity was meant to emulate the life and ministry of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"). Franciscans traveled and preached in the streets, while staying in church properties. Clare, under Francis's guidance, founded the Poor Clares (Order of Saint Clare) of the Franciscans.

The extreme poverty required of members was relaxed in the final revision of the rule in 1223. The degree of observance required of members remained a major source of conflict within the order, resulting in numerous secessions.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The Order of Friars Minor, previously known as the "Observant" branch, is one of the three Franciscan [First Orders](/wiki/Religious_institute#Categorization "Religious institute") within the Catholic Church, the others being the "[Conventuals](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual")", formed in 1209, and the "[Capuchins](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin")", founded in 1520.

The Order of Friars Minor in its current form is the result of an amalgamation of several smaller orders completed in 1897 by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII").[[7]](#cite_note-OFM_CE1913-7) The Capuchins and Conventuals remain distinct [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") within the Catholic Church, observing the Rule of Saint Francis with different emphases. Conventual Franciscans are sometimes referred to as **minorites** or **greyfriars** because of their [habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit"). In Poland and Lithuania they are known as **[Bernardines](/wiki/Bernardines_(Franciscans) "Bernardines (Franciscans)")**, after [Bernardino of Siena](/wiki/Bernardino_of_Siena "Bernardino of Siena"), although the term elsewhere refers to [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians") instead.

## Name and demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name and demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/StFrancis_part.jpg/250px-StFrancis_part.jpg)](/wiki/File:StFrancis_part.jpg)

Francis of Assisi, founder of the Order of Friars Minor. The oldest known portrait in existence of Francis, dating back to his retreat to [Subiaco](/wiki/Subiaco,_Lazio "Subiaco, Lazio"), 1223–1224.

The name of the original order, *Ordo Fratrum Minorum* ([Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor"), literally 'Order of Lesser Brothers') stems from [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi")'s rejection of luxury and wealth. Francis was the son of a rich cloth merchant but gave up his wealth to pursue his faith more fully. He had cut all ties that remained with his family and pursued a life living in solidarity with his fellow brothers in Christ.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In other words, he abandoned his life among the wealthy and aristocratic classes (or *majori*) to live like the poor and peasants (*minori*). Francis adopted the simple tunic worn by peasants as the [religious habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit") for his order and had others who wished to join him do the same. Those who joined him became the original Order of Friars Minor.[[1]](#cite_note-sbf-1)

### First Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First Order")]

The First Order or the Order of Friars Minor, or **Seraphic Order[[9]](#cite_note-9)** are commonly called simply the **Franciscans**. This order is a [mendicant](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") [religious order](/wiki/Religious_order_(Catholic) "Religious order (Catholic)") of men, some of whom trace their origin to Francis of Assisi.[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10) Their official [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name is the *Ordo Fratrum Minorum*.[[11]](#cite_note-ceofm-11) Francis thus referred to his followers as "[Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli")", meaning "Little Brothers". Franciscan brothers are informally called **friars** or the **Minorites**.[[12]](#cite_note-cefran-12)

The modern organization of the Friars Minor comprises three separate families or groups, each considered a religious order in its own right under its own minister general and particular type of governance. They all live according to a body of regulations known as the [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis").[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10)

* The [Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor"), also known as the Observants, are most commonly simply called **Franciscan friars**,[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10) official name: Friars Minor (OFM).[[12]](#cite_note-cefran-12)
* The [Order of Friars Minor Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") or simply **Capuchins**,[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10) official name: Friars Minor Capuchin (OFM Cap.).[[12]](#cite_note-cefran-12)
* The [Order of Friars Minor Conventual](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") or simply **Minorites**,[[10]](#cite_note-Fordham-10) official name: Friars Minor Conventual (OFM Conv.).[[12]](#cite_note-cefran-12)

### Second Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Second Order")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Ribalta-san_francisco-prado.jpg/250px-Ribalta-san_francisco-prado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ribalta-san_francisco-prado.jpg)

*[Saint Francis](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") Comforted by a Musician Angel*, by [Francisco Ribalta](/wiki/Francesc_Ribalta "Francesc Ribalta")

The Second Order, most commonly called **[Poor Clares](/wiki/Poor_Clares "Poor Clares")** in English-speaking countries, consists of one branch of religious sisters. The order is called the Order of St. Clare (OSC). Prior to 1263 they were called "The Poor Ladies", "The Poor Enclosed Nuns", and "The Order of San Damiano".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Third Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Third Order")]

The Franciscan [third order](/wiki/Third_order "Third order"), known as the [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis"), has many men and women members, separated into two main branches:

* The **[Secular Franciscan Order](/wiki/Secular_Franciscan_Order "Secular Franciscan Order")**, OFS, originally known as the Brothers and Sisters of Penance or Third Order of Penance, try to live the ideals of the movement in their daily lives outside of [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute").
* Members of the **[Third Order Regular](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis#Third_Order_Regular "Third Order of Saint Francis")** (TOR) live in religious communities under the traditional [religious vows](/wiki/Religious_vows "Religious vows"). They grew out of what became the Secular Franciscan Order.

### Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Membership")]

The 2013 *[Annuario Pontificio](/wiki/Annuario_Pontificio "Annuario Pontificio")* gave the following figures for the membership of the principal male Franciscan orders:.[[14]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-14)

* OFM: 1,915 communities. 12,476 members, including 8,512 priests.
* OFM Conv.: 572 communities. 3,981 members, including 2,777 priests.
* OFM Cap.: 1,542 communities. 10,355 members, including 6,796 priests.
* TOR: 147 communities. 813 members, including 581 priests.

The coat of arms that is a universal symbol of Franciscan "contains the [Tau cross](/wiki/Tau_cross "Tau cross"), with two crossed arms: Christ's right hand with the nail wound and Francis' left hand with the [stigmata](/wiki/Stigmata "Stigmata") wound."[[1]](#cite_note-sbf-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Regra_bulada.jpg/250px-Regra_bulada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Regra_bulada.jpg)

*[Regula bullata](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis")*, the rule confirmed by [Pope Honorius III](/wiki/Pope_Honorius_III "Pope Honorius III")

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

In 1209, a sermon Francis heard on [Matthew 10:9](/wiki/Matthew_10:9 "Matthew 10:9") made such an impression on him that he decided to devote himself wholly to a life of apostolic poverty. Clad in a rough garment, barefoot, and, after the [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") precept, without staff or scrip, he began to preach repentance.[[15]](#cite_note-cefa-15)

He was soon joined by a prominent fellow townsman, [Bernard of Quintavalle](/wiki/Bernard_of_Quintavalle "Bernard of Quintavalle"), who contributed all that he had to the work. Other companions joined, with Francis having 11 companions within a year. The brothers lived in the deserted [leper colony](/wiki/Leper_colony "Leper colony") of Rivo Torto near [Assisi](/wiki/Assisi "Assisi"). They spent much of their time traveling through the mountainous districts of [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria"), always cheerful and full of songs, making a deep impression on their hearers by their earnest exhortations. Their life was extremely [ascetic](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism"). Probably as early as 1209, Francis gave them a first rule, a collection of Scriptural passages emphasizing the duty of poverty.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In spite of some similarities between this principle and some of the fundamental ideas of the followers of [Peter Waldo](/wiki/Peter_Waldo "Peter Waldo"), the brotherhood of Assisi succeeded in gaining the approval of [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III").[[17]](#cite_note-chest107-17) What seems to have impressed first the [Bishop of Assisi](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Assisi-Nocera_Umbra-Gualdo_Tadino "Roman Catholic Diocese of Assisi-Nocera Umbra-Gualdo Tadino"), then [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") [Giovanni di San Paolo](/wiki/Giovanni_di_San_Paolo "Giovanni di San Paolo") and finally Innocent, was their utter loyalty to the Catholic Church and the clergy. Pope Innocent was responsible for helping to construct the church Francis was being called to rebuild. Innocent and the [Fourth Lateran Council](/wiki/Fourth_Council_of_the_Lateran "Fourth Council of the Lateran") helped maintain the church in Europe.[[18]](#cite_note-Francis_of_Assisi_and_His_World-18)

Pope Innocent probably saw in them a possible answer to his desire for an orthodox preaching force to counter heresy. Many legends have clustered around the decisive audience of Francis with the pope. The realistic account in [Matthew Paris](/wiki/Matthew_Paris "Matthew Paris")—according to which the pope originally sent the shabby saint off to keep swine and only recognized his real worth by his ready obedience—has, in spite of its improbability, a certain historical interest since it shows the natural antipathy of the older [Benedictine monasticism](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict "Order of Saint Benedict") to the plebeian mendicant orders. The group was [tonsured](/wiki/Tonsure "Tonsure"), and Francis was ordained as a deacon, allowing him to proclaim Gospel passages and preach in churches during Mass.[[18]](#cite_note-Francis_of_Assisi_and_His_World-18)

### Francis's last years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Francis's last years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Dream_of_Innocent_III_and_the_Confirmation_of_the_Rule_of_St_Francis_Benozzo_Gozzoli.jpg/250px-Dream_of_Innocent_III_and_the_Confirmation_of_the_Rule_of_St_Francis_Benozzo_Gozzoli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dream_of_Innocent_III_and_the_Confirmation_of_the_Rule_of_St_Francis_Benozzo_Gozzoli.jpg)

A dream of [Innocent III](/wiki/Innocent_III "Innocent III") and the Confirmation of the [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis"), [Benozzo Gozzoli](/wiki/Benozzo_Gozzoli "Benozzo Gozzoli")

in 1219, after intense apostolic activity in Italy, Francis went to Egypt with the [Fifth Crusade](/wiki/Fifth_Crusade "Fifth Crusade") to announce the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") to the [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens "Saracens"). He met with the Sultan [Malik al-Kamil](/wiki/Al-Kamil "Al-Kamil"), initiating a spirit of dialogue and understanding between [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). The Franciscan presence in the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land") started in 1217, when the province of [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") was established, with Brother Elias as minister. By 1229, the friars had a small house near the fifth station of the [Via Dolorosa](/wiki/Via_Dolorosa "Via Dolorosa"). In 1272, Sultan [Baibars](/wiki/Baibars "Baibars") allowed the Franciscans to settle in the [Cenacle](/wiki/Cenacle "Cenacle") on [Mount Zion](/wiki/Mount_Zion "Mount Zion").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1309, they also settled in the [Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre") and in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"). In 1335, the king of Naples [Robert of Anjou](/wiki/Robert,_King_of_Naples "Robert, King of Naples") ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Roberto d'Angiò*) and his wife [Sancha of Majorca](/wiki/Sancha_of_Majorca "Sancha of Majorca") ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Sancia di Maiorca*) bought the Cenacle and gave it to the Franciscans. In 1342, [Pope Clement VI](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VI "Pope Clement VI") by the Bulls *Gratias agimus* and *Nuper charissimae*, declared the Franciscans as the official custodians of the Holy Places in the name of the Catholic Church. The Franciscan [Custody of the Holy Land](/wiki/Custody_of_the_Holy_Land "Custody of the Holy Land") is still in force today.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The controversy about how to follow the Gospel life of poverty, which extends through the first three centuries of Franciscan history, began in Francis' lifetime. The ascetic brothers Matthew of [Narni](/wiki/Narni "Narni") and Gregory of Naples, a nephew of Cardinal Ugolino, were the two vicars-general to whom Francis had entrusted the direction of the order during his time in Egypt. They carried through at a chapter which they held certain stricter regulations in regard to fasting and the reception of alms, which departed from the spirit of the original rule. It did not take Francis long, on his return, to suppress this insubordinate tendency.

He was less successful in regard to another of an opposite nature which soon came up. [Elias of Cortona](/wiki/Elias_of_Cortona "Elias of Cortona") originated a movement for the increase of the worldly consideration of the order and the adaptation of its system to the plans of the hierarchy. This conflicted with the original notions of Francis and helped to bring about the successive changes in the rule already described. Francis was not alone in opposition to this lax and secularizing tendency. On the contrary, the party which clung to his original views and after his death took his "testament" for their guide, known as Observantists or *[Zelanti](/wiki/Zelanti "Zelanti")*, was at least equal in numbers and activity to the followers of Elias.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Honorius_III_approving_the_Rule_of_St._Francis%2C_Bartolome_del_Castro%2C_c._1500_%28Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art%29.jpg/250px-Honorius_III_approving_the_Rule_of_St._Francis%2C_Bartolome_del_Castro%2C_c._1500_%28Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Honorius_III_approving_the_Rule_of_St._Francis,_Bartolome_del_Castro,_c._1500_(Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art).jpg)

*[Honorius III](/wiki/Honorius_III "Honorius III") Approving the Rule of St. [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi")*, Bartolome del Castro, c. 1500, [Philadelphia Museum of Art](/wiki/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art "Philadelphia Museum of Art")

In 1219, exasperated by the demands of running a growing and fractious order, Francis asked [Pope Honorius III](/wiki/Pope_Honorius_III "Pope Honorius III") for help. He was assigned Cardinal [Ugolino](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") as protector of the order by the pope. Francis resigned the day-to-day running of the order. Francis retained the power to shape legislation, writing a rule in 1221 which he revised and had approved in 1223. After about 1221, the day-to-day running of the order was in the hands of Brother [Elias of Cortona](/wiki/Elias_of_Cortona "Elias of Cortona"), who was elected as leader of the friars a few years after Francis's death in 1232 but who aroused much opposition because of his autocratic leadership style.[[21]](#cite_note-Paschal-21) He planned and built the [Basilica of San Francesco d'Assisi](/wiki/Basilica_of_San_Francesco_d%27Assisi "Basilica of San Francesco d'Assisi") in which Francis is buried, a building which includes the friary [Sacro Convento](/wiki/Sacro_Convento "Sacro Convento"), still today the spiritual centre of the order.[[22]](#cite_note-sacro-22)

In the external successes of the brothers, as they were reported at the yearly general chapters, there was much to encourage Francis. [Caesar of Speyer](/wiki/Caesar_of_Speyer "Caesar of Speyer"), the first German [provincial](/wiki/Provincial_superior "Provincial superior"), a zealous advocate of the founder's strict principle of poverty, began in 1221 from [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") with 25 companions, to win for the order in the region of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") and the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"). In 1224, [Agnellus of Pisa](/wiki/Agnellus_of_Pisa "Agnellus of Pisa") led a small group of friars to England. The branch arriving in England became known as the "greyfriars".[[23]](#cite_note-23) Beginning at [Greyfriars](/wiki/Greyfriars,_Canterbury "Greyfriars, Canterbury") at [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury"), the ecclesiastical capital, they moved on to [London](/wiki/London "London"), the political capital, and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), the intellectual capital. From these three bases, the Franciscans swiftly expanded, to embrace the principal towns of England.

### Development after Francis's death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Development after Francis's death")]

#### 1232–1239

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1232–1239")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Anthony_pereda.jpg/250px-Anthony_pereda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthony_pereda.jpg)

[Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua") (c. 1195–1231) with the [Infant Christ](/wiki/Child_Jesus "Child Jesus"), painting by [Antonio de Pereda](/wiki/Antonio_de_Pereda "Antonio de Pereda") (c. 1611–1678)

Elias was a lay friar, and encouraged other laymen to enter the order. This brought opposition from many ordained friars and ministers provincial, who also opposed increased centralization of the Order. [Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") declared his intention to build a splendid church to house the body of Francis and the task fell to Elias, who at once began to lay plans for the erection of a great basilica at Assisi, to enshrine the remains of the *Poverello*.[[21]](#cite_note-Paschal-21) In order to build the basilica, Elias proceeded to collect money in various ways to meet the expenses of the building. Elias thus also alienated the zealots in the order, who felt this was not in keeping with the founder's views upon the question of poverty.

The earliest leader of the strict party was [Brother Leo](/wiki/Brother_Leo "Brother Leo"), a close companion of Francis during his last years and the author of the *Speculum perfectionis*, a strong polemic against the laxer party. Having protested against the collection of money for the erection of the basilica of San Francesco, it was Leo who broke in pieces the marble box which Elias had set up for offertories for the completion of the basilica at [Assisi](/wiki/Assisi "Assisi"). For this Elias had him scourged, and this outrage on St Francis's dearest disciple consolidated the opposition to Elias. Leo was the leader in the early stages of the struggle in the order for the maintenance of St Francis's ideas on strict poverty.[[24]](#cite_note-paschrob-24) At the chapter held in May 1227, Elias was rejected in spite of his prominence, and [Giovanni Parenti](/wiki/Giovanni_Parenti "Giovanni Parenti"), Minister Provincial of Spain, was elected Minister General of the order.

In 1232 Elias succeeded him, and under him the Order significantly developed its ministries and presence in the towns. Many new houses were founded, especially in Italy, and in many of them special attention was paid to education. The somewhat earlier settlements of Franciscan teachers at the universities (in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), for example, where [Alexander of Hales](/wiki/Alexander_of_Hales "Alexander of Hales") was teaching) continued to develop. Contributions toward the promotion of the Order's work, and especially the building of the Basilica in Assisi, came in abundantly. Funds could only be accepted on behalf of the friars for determined, imminent, real necessities that could not be provided for from begging. When in 1230, the General Chapter could not agree on a common interpretation of the 1223 Rule it sent a delegation including [Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua") to [Pope Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") for an authentic interpretation of this piece of papal legislation. The bull *Quo elongati* of Gregory IX declared that the Testament of St. Francis was not legally binding and offered an interpretation of poverty that would allow the Order to continue to develop. Gregory IX authorized agents of the Order to have custody of such funds where they could not be spent immediately. Elias pursued with great severity the principal leaders of the opposition, and even [Bernardo di Quintavalle](/wiki/Bernardo_di_Quintavalle "Bernardo di Quintavalle"), the founder's first disciple, was obliged to conceal himself for years in the forest of [Monte Sefro](/wiki/Sefro "Sefro").

The conflict between the two parties lasted many years and the *Zelanti* won several notable victories in spite of the favor shown to their opponents by the papal administration, until finally the reconciliation of the two points of view was seen to be impossible and the order was actually split into halves.

#### 1239–1274

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1239–1274")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Pal%C3%A1cio_Nacional_de_Mafra_%281%29.jpg/250px-Pal%C3%A1cio_Nacional_de_Mafra_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pal%C3%A1cio_Nacional_de_Mafra_(1).jpg)

A Franciscan convent in [Mafra](/wiki/Mafra,_Portugal "Mafra, Portugal") in Portugal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Claude_%28dit_Fr%C3%A8re_Luc%29_-_Saint_Bonaventure.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois%2C_Claude_%28dit_Fr%C3%A8re_Luc%29_-_Saint_Bonaventure.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois,_Claude_(dit_Fr%C3%A8re_Luc)_-_Saint_Bonaventure.jpg)

[Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure") (1221–1274), painting by Claude François (c. 1650–1660)

Elias governed the Order from the center, imposing his authority on the provinces (as had Francis). A reaction to this centralized government was led from the provinces of England and Germany. At the general chapter of 1239, held in Rome under the personal presidency of Gregory IX, Elias was deposed in favor of [Albert of Pisa](/wiki/Albert_of_Pisa "Albert of Pisa"), the former provincial of England,[[25]](#cite_note-new_advent_friars-25) a moderate Observantist. This chapter introduced General Statutes to govern the Order and devolved power from the [Minister General](/wiki/Minister_General_(Franciscan) "Minister General (Franciscan)") to the Ministers Provincial sitting in chapter. The next two Ministers General, [Haymo of Faversham](/wiki/Haymo_of_Faversham "Haymo of Faversham") (1240–1244) and [Crescentius of Jesi](/wiki/Crescentius_of_Jesi "Crescentius of Jesi") (1244–1247), consolidated this greater democracy in the Order but also led the Order towards a greater clericalization. The new [Pope Innocent IV](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_IV "Pope Innocent IV") supported them in this. In a bull of November 14, 1245, this pope even sanctioned an extension of the system of financial agents, and allowed the funds to be used not simply for those things that were necessary for the friars but also for those that were useful.

The Observantist party took a strong stand in opposition to this ruling and agitated so successfully against the lax General that in 1247, at a chapter held in Lyon, France—where Innocent IV was then residing—he was replaced by the strict Observantist [John of Parma](/wiki/John_of_Parma "John of Parma") (1247–1257) and the Order refused to implement any provisions of Innocent IV that were laxer than those of Gregory IX.

Elias, who had been excommunicated and taken under the protection of [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"), was now forced to give up all hope of recovering his power in the Order. He died in 1253, after succeeding by recantation in obtaining the removal of his censures. Under John of Parma, who enjoyed the favor of Innocent IV and [Pope Alexander IV](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_IV "Pope Alexander IV"), the influence of the Order was notably increased, especially by the provisions of the latter pope in regard to the academic activity of the brothers. He not only sanctioned the theological institutes in Franciscan houses, but did all he could to support the friars in the Mendicant Controversy, when the secular Masters of the [University of Paris](/wiki/University_of_Paris "University of Paris") and the Bishops of France combined to attack the [mendicant orders](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders"). It was due to the action of [Alexander IV](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_IV "Pope Alexander IV")'s envoys, who were obliged to threaten the university authorities with excommunication, that the degree of doctor of theology was finally conceded to the [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") and the Franciscan [Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure") (1257), who had previously been able to lecture only as licentiates.

The Franciscan [Gerard of Borgo San Donnino](/wiki/Gerard_of_Borgo_San_Donnino "Gerard of Borgo San Donnino") at this time issued a Joachimite tract and [John of Parma](/wiki/John_of_Parma "John of Parma") was seen as favoring the condemned theology of [Joachim of Fiore](/wiki/Joachim_of_Fiore "Joachim of Fiore"). To protect the Order from its enemies, John was forced to step down and recommended Bonaventure as his successor. Bonaventure saw the need to unify the Order around a common ideology and both wrote a new life of the founder and collected the Order's legislation into the [Constitutions of Narbonne](/w/index.php?title=Constitutions_of_Narbonne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Constitutions of Narbonne (page does not exist)"), so called because they were ratified by the Order at its chapter held at [Narbonne](/wiki/Narbonne "Narbonne"), France, in 1260. In the chapter of [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa") three years later Bonaventure's *Legenda maior* was approved as the only biography of Francis and all previous biographies were ordered to be destroyed. Bonaventure ruled (1257–1274) in a moderate spirit, which is represented also by various works produced by the order in his time – especially by the *Expositio regulae* written by [David of Augsburg](/wiki/David_of_Augsburg "David of Augsburg") soon after 1260.

### 14th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 14th century")]

#### 1274–1300

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1274–1300")]

See also: [Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli")

The successor to Bonaventure, Jerome of Ascoli or Girolamo Masci (1274–1279), (the future [Pope Nicholas IV](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_IV "Pope Nicholas IV")), and his successor, [Bonagratia of Bologna](/wiki/Bonagratia_of_Bologna "Bonagratia of Bologna") (1279–1285), also followed a middle course. Severe measures were taken against certain extreme [Spirituals](/w/index.php?title=Spiritual_Franciscanism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Spiritual Franciscanism (page does not exist)") who, on the strength of the rumor that [Pope Gregory X](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_X "Pope Gregory X") was intending at the [Council of Lyon](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Lyon "Second Council of Lyon") (1274–1275) to force the mendicant orders to tolerate the possession of property, threatened both pope and council with the renunciation of allegiance. Attempts were made, however, to satisfy the reasonable demands of the Spiritual party, as in the bull *Exiit qui seminat*[[26]](#cite_note-26) of [Pope Nicholas III](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_III "Pope Nicholas III") (1279), which pronounced the principle of complete poverty to be meritorious and holy, but interpreted it in the way of a somewhat sophistical distinction between possession and usufruct. The bull was received respectfully by Bonagratia and the next two generals, [Arlotto of Prato](/wiki/Arlotto_of_Prato "Arlotto of Prato") (1285–1287) and [Matthew of Aqua Sparta](/wiki/Matthew_of_Aqua_Sparta "Matthew of Aqua Sparta") (1287–1289); but the Spiritual party under the leadership of the Bonaventuran pupil and apocalyptic [Pierre Jean Olivi](/wiki/Peter_Olivi "Peter Olivi") regarded its provisions for the dependence of the friars upon the pope and the division between brothers occupied in manual labor and those employed on spiritual missions as a corruption of the fundamental principles of the Order. They were not won over by the conciliatory attitude of the next general, [Raymond Gaufredi](/wiki/Raymond_Gaufredi "Raymond Gaufredi") (1289–1296), and of the Franciscan Pope Nicholas IV (1288–1292). The attempt made by the next pope, [Celestine V](/wiki/Pope_Celestine_V "Pope Celestine V"), an old friend of the order, to end the strife by uniting the Observantist party with his own order of hermits (see [Celestines](/wiki/Celestines "Celestines")) was scarcely more successful. Only a part of the Spirituals joined the new order, and the secession scarcely lasted beyond the reign of the hermit-pope. [Pope Boniface VIII](/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII "Pope Boniface VIII") annulled Celestine's bull of foundation with his other acts, deposed the general [Raymond Gaufredi](/wiki/Raymond_de_Gaufredi "Raymond de Gaufredi"), and appointed a man of laxer tendency, [John de Murro](/wiki/John_de_Murro "John de Murro"), in his place. The Benedictine section of the Celestines was separated from the Franciscan section, and the latter was formally suppressed by Pope Boniface VIII in 1302. The leader of the Observantists, Olivi, who spent his last years in the Franciscan house at Tarnius and died there in 1298, had pronounced against the more extreme "Spiritual" attitude, and given an exposition of the theory of poverty which was approved by the more moderate Observantists, and for a long time constituted their principle.

#### Persecution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Persecution")]

Under [Pope Clement V](/wiki/Pope_Clement_V "Pope Clement V") (1305–1314) this party succeeded in exercising some influence on papal decisions. In 1309, Clement had a commission sit at [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon") for the purpose of reconciling the conflicting parties. [Ubertino of Casale](/wiki/Ubertino_of_Casale "Ubertino of Casale"), the leader, after Olivi's death, of the stricter party, who was a member of the commission, induced the [Council of Vienne](/wiki/Council_of_Vienne "Council of Vienne") to arrive at a decision in the main favoring his views. The 1313 papal constitution *Exivi de paradiso* was on the whole conceived in the same sense.

Clement's successor, [Pope John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII") (1316–1334), favored the laxer or conventual party. By the bull *Quorundam exigit* he modified several provisions of the constitution *Exivi*, and required the formal submission of the Spirituals. Some of them, encouraged by the strongly Observantist general [Michael of Cesena](/wiki/Michael_of_Cesena "Michael of Cesena"), ventured to dispute the pope's right so to deal with the provisions of his predecessor. Sixty-four of them were summoned to Avignon and the most obstinate delivered over to the Inquisition, four of them being burned in 1318. Shortly before this, all the separate houses of the Observantists had been suppressed.

#### Renewed controversy on the question of poverty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Renewed controversy on the question of poverty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Panewniki_Hedwig.jpg/250px-Panewniki_Hedwig.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panewniki_Hedwig.jpg)

Franciscan friary in [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice"), Poland

A few years later a new controversy, this time theoretical, broke out on the question of [poverty](/wiki/Apostolic_poverty "Apostolic poverty"). In his 14 August 1279 bull *Exiit qui seminat*,[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Pope Nicholas III](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_III "Pope Nicholas III") had confirmed the arrangement already established by [Pope Innocent IV](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_IV "Pope Innocent IV"), by which all property given to the Franciscans was vested in the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), which granted the friars the mere use of it. The bull declared that renunciation of ownership of all things "both individually but also in common, for God's sake, is meritorious and holy; Christ, also, showing the way of perfection, taught it by word and confirmed it by example, and the first founders of the church militant, as they had drawn it from the fountainhead itself, distributed it through the channels of their teaching and life to those wishing to live perfectly."[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Schatz-30)

Although *Exiit qui seminat* banned the disputation of its contents, the decades that followed saw increasingly bitter disputes about the form of poverty to be observed by Franciscans, with the Spirituals (so called because associated with the Age of the Spirit that [Joachim of Fiore](/wiki/Joachim_of_Fiore "Joachim of Fiore") had said would begin in 1260)[[31]](#cite_note-History-31) pitched against the [Conventual Franciscans](/wiki/Conventual_Franciscans "Conventual Franciscans").[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Pope Clement V](/wiki/Pope_Clement_V "Pope Clement V")'s bull *Exivi de Paradiso* of 20 November 1312[[33]](#cite_note-33) failed to effect a compromise between the two factions.[[31]](#cite_note-History-31) Clement V's successor, [Pope John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII") was determined to suppress what he considered to be the excesses of the Spirituals, who contended eagerly for the view that Christ and his apostles had possessed absolutely nothing, either separately or jointly, and who were citing *Exiit qui seminat* in support of their view.[[34]](#cite_note-Kleinhenz-34)

In 1317, John XXII formally condemned the group of them known as the Fraticelli.[[31]](#cite_note-History-31) On 26 March 1322, with *Quia nonnunquam*, he removed the ban on discussion of Nicholas III's bull[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) and commissioned experts to examine the idea of poverty based on belief that Christ and the apostles owned nothing. The experts disagreed among themselves, but the majority condemned the idea on the grounds that it would condemn the church's right to have possessions.[[31]](#cite_note-History-31) The Franciscan chapter held in [Perugia](/wiki/Perugia "Perugia") in May 1322 declared on the contrary: "To say or assert that Christ, in showing the way of perfection, and the Apostles, in following that way and setting an example to others who wished to lead the perfect life, possessed nothing either severally or in common, either by right of ownership and *dominium* or by personal right, we corporately and unanimously declare to be not heretical, but true and catholic."[[31]](#cite_note-History-31)

By the bull *Ad conditorem canonum* of 8 December 1322,[[37]](#cite_note-37) John XXII, declaring it ridiculous to pretend that every scrap of food given to the friars and eaten by them belonged to the pope, refused to accept ownership over the goods of the Franciscans in the future and granted them exemption from the rule that absolutely forbade ownership of anything even in common, thus forcing them to accept ownership.[[38]](#cite_note-38) And, on 12 November 1323, he issued the short bull *Quum inter nonnullos*[[39]](#cite_note-39) which declared "erroneous and heretical" the doctrine that Christ and his apostles had no possessions whatever.[[30]](#cite_note-Schatz-30)[[34]](#cite_note-Kleinhenz-34)[[40]](#cite_note-40) John XXII's actions thus demolished the fictitious structure that gave the appearance of absolute poverty to the life of the Franciscan friars.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Influential members of the order protested, such as the minister general [Michael of Cesena](/wiki/Michael_of_Cesena "Michael of Cesena"), the English provincial [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham"), and [Bonagratia of Bergamo](/wiki/Bonagratia_of_Bergamo "Bonagratia of Bergamo"). In 1324, [Louis the Bavarian](/wiki/Louis_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Louis IV, Holy Roman Emperor") sided with the Spirituals and accused the pope of heresy. In reply to the argument of his opponents that Nicholas III's bull *Exiit qui seminat* was fixed and irrevocable, John XXII issued the bull *Quia quorundam* on 10 November 1324[[42]](#cite_note-42) in which he declared that it cannot be inferred from the words of the 1279 bull that Christ and the apostles had nothing, adding: "Indeed, it can be inferred rather that the Gospel life lived by Christ and the Apostles did not exclude some possessions in common, since living 'without property' does not require that those living thus should have nothing in common." In 1328, Michael of Cesena was summoned to Avignon to explain the Order's intransigence in refusing the pope's orders and its complicity with Louis of Bavaria. Michael was imprisoned in Avignon, together with Francesco d'Ascoli, Bonagratia, and William of Ockham. In January of that year Louis of Bavaria entered Rome and had himself crowned emperor. Three months later he declared John XXII deposed and installed the Spiritual Franciscan [Pietro Rainalducci](/wiki/Antipope_Nicholas_V "Antipope Nicholas V") as [antipope](/wiki/Antipope "Antipope"). The Franciscan chapter that opened in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") on 28 May reelected Michael of Cesena, who two days before had escaped with his companions from Avignon. But in August Louis the Bavarian and his pope had to flee Rome before an attack by [Robert, King of Naples](/wiki/Robert,_King_of_Naples "Robert, King of Naples"). Only a small part of the Franciscan Order joined the opponents of John XXII, and at a general chapter held in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in 1329 the majority of all the houses declared their submission to the Pope. With the bull *Quia vir reprobus* of 16 November 1329,[[43]](#cite_note-43) John XXII replied to Michael of Cesena's attacks on *Ad conditorem canonum*, *Quum inter nonnullos*, and *Quia quorundam*. In 1330, Antipope Nicholas V submitted, followed later by the ex-general Michael, and finally, just before his death, by Ockham.[[31]](#cite_note-History-31)

### Separate congregations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Separate congregations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/A_christian_missionary_Friar_landing_in_southrn_India_%2814th_cCentury%29.jpg/250px-A_christian_missionary_Friar_landing_in_southrn_India_%2814th_cCentury%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_christian_missionary_Friar_landing_in_southrn_India_(14th_cCentury).jpg)

A Christian missionary friar landing in southern India (14th century)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG/250px-Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG)](/wiki/File:Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG)

Franciscan convent at Lopud in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")

Out of all these dissensions in the 14th century sprang a number of separate congregations, or almost sects, to say nothing of the heretical parties of the [Beghards](/wiki/Beghards "Beghards") and [Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli"), some of which developed within the Order on both hermit and cenobitic principles and may here be mentioned:

#### Clareni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Clareni")]

The Clareni or Clarenini was an association of hermits established on the river Clareno in the march of [Ancona](/wiki/Ancona "Ancona") by [Angelo da Clareno](/wiki/Angelo_da_Clareno "Angelo da Clareno") (1337). Like several other smaller congregations, it was obliged in 1568 under [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope_Pius_V "Pope Pius V") to unite with the general body of Observantists.

#### Minorites of Narbonne

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Minorites of Narbonne")]

As a separate congregation, this originated through the union of a number of houses which followed Olivi after 1308. It was limited to southwestern France and, its members being accused of the heresy of the Beghards, was suppressed by the Inquisition during the controversies under John XXII.

#### Reform of Johannes de Vallibus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Reform of Johannes de Vallibus")]

This was founded in the hermitage of St. Bartholomew at Brugliano near [Foligno](/wiki/Foligno "Foligno") in 1334. The congregation was suppressed by the Franciscan general chapter in 1354; reestablished in 1368 by Paolo de' Trinci of Foligno; confirmed by Gregory XI in 1373, and spread rapidly from Central Italy to France, Spain, Hungary, and elsewhere. Most of the Observantist houses joined this congregation by degrees, so that it became known simply as the "brothers of the regular Observance."

It acquired the favor of the popes by its energetic opposition to the heretical [Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli"), and was expressly recognized by the 1415 [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance"). It was allowed to have a special vicar-general of its own and legislate for its members without reference to the conventual part of the Order. Through the work of such men as [Bernardino of Siena](/wiki/Bernardino_of_Siena "Bernardino of Siena"), [Giovanni da Capistrano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Capistrano "Giovanni da Capistrano"), and [Dietrich Coelde](/wiki/Dietrich_Coelde "Dietrich Coelde") (b. 1435? at Munster; was a member of the [Brethren of the Common Life](/wiki/Brethren_of_the_Common_Life "Brethren of the Common Life"), died December 11, 1515), it gained great prominence during the 15th century. By the end of the Middle Ages, the Observantists, with 1,400 houses, comprised nearly half of the entire Order.

Their influence brought about attempts at reform even among the Conventuals, including the quasi-Observantist brothers living under the rule of the Conventual ministers (Martinianists or *Observantes sub ministris*), such as the male Colletans, later led by Boniface de Ceva in his reform attempts principally in France and Germany; the reformed congregation founded in 1426 by the Spaniard Philip de Berbegal and distinguished by the special importance they attached to the little hood (*cappuciola*); the Neutri, a group of reformers originating about 1463 in Italy, who tried to take a middle ground between the Conventuals and Observantists, but refused to obey the heads of either, until they were compelled by the pope to affiliate with the regular Observantists, or with those of the Common Life; the Caperolani, a congregation founded about 1470 in North Italy by [Peter Caperolo](/wiki/Pietro_Caperolo "Pietro Caperolo"), but dissolved again on the death of its founder in 1481; the Amadeists, founded by the noble Portuguese Amadeo, who entered the Franciscan order at Assisi in 1452, gathered around him a number of adherents to his fairly strict principles (numbering finally twenty-six houses), and died in the odor of sanctity in 1482.

#### Unification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Unification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Ko%C5%9Bci%C3%B3%C5%82_%C5%9Bw._Barbary_w_Przeworsku_fasada1.jpg/250px-Ko%C5%9Bci%C3%B3%C5%82_%C5%9Bw._Barbary_w_Przeworsku_fasada1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ko%C5%9Bci%C3%B3%C5%82_%C5%9Bw._Barbary_w_Przeworsku_fasada1.jpg)

A 15th century Franciscan church in [Przeworsk](/wiki/Przeworsk "Przeworsk"), Poland

Projects for a union between the two main branches of the Order were put forth not only by the Council of Constance but by several popes, without any positive result. By direction of [Pope Martin V](/wiki/Pope_Martin_V "Pope Martin V"), [John of Capistrano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Capistrano "Giovanni da Capistrano") drew up statutes which were to serve as a basis for reunion, and they were actually accepted by a general chapter at Assisi in 1430; but the majority of the Conventual houses refused to agree to them, and they remained without effect. At [John of Capistrano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Capistrano "Giovanni da Capistrano")'s request [Eugene IV](/wiki/Pope_Eugene_IV "Pope Eugene IV") issued a bull (*Ut sacra minorum*, 1446) aimed at the same result, but again nothing was accomplished.

Equally unsuccessful were the attempts of the Franciscan [Pope Sixtus IV](/wiki/Pope_Sixtus_IV "Pope Sixtus IV"), who bestowed a vast number of privileges on both of the original mendicant orders, but by this very fact lost the favor of the Observants and failed in his plans for reunion. [Julius II](/wiki/Pope_Julius_II "Pope Julius II") succeeded in reducing some of the smaller branches, but left the division of the two great parties untouched. This division was legalized by [Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X"), after a general chapter held in Rome in 1517, in connection with the reform-movement of the [Fifth Lateran Council](/wiki/Fifth_Council_of_the_Lateran "Fifth Council of the Lateran"), had once more declared the impossibility of reunion. The less strict principles of the Conventuals, permitting the possession of real estate and the enjoyment of fixed revenues, were recognized as tolerable, while the Observants, in contrast to this *usus moderatus*, were held strictly to their own *usus arctus* or *pauper*.

All of the groups that followed the Franciscan Rule literally were united to the Observants, and the right to elect the [Minister General of the Order](/wiki/Minister_General_(Franciscan) "Minister General (Franciscan)"), together with the seal of the Order, was given to this united grouping.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] This grouping, since it adhered more closely to the rule of the founder, was allowed to claim a certain superiority over the Conventuals. The Observant general, elected now for six years, not for life, inherited the title of "Minister-General of the Whole Order of St. Francis". He was granted the right to confirm the choice of a head for the Conventuals, who was known as "Master-General of the Friars Minor Conventual"—although this privilege never became practically operative.

### Franciscans and the Inquisition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Franciscans and the Inquisition")]

Main articles: [Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition") and [Medieval Inquisition § Papal inquisition](/wiki/Medieval_Inquisition#Papal_inquisition "Medieval Inquisition")

In about 1236 during the time of [Elias of Cortona](/wiki/Elias_of_Cortona "Elias of Cortona"), [Pope Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") appointed the Franciscans, along with the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"), as Inquisitors.[[44]](#cite_note-auto-44) The Franciscans had been involved in anti-heretical activities from the beginning simply by preaching and acting as living examples of the Gospel life.[[45]](#cite_note-45) As official Inquisitors, they were authorized to use torture to extract confessions, as approved by [Pope Innocent IV](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_IV "Pope Innocent IV") in 1252 while [John of Parma](/wiki/John_of_Parma "John of Parma") was General Minister.[[44]](#cite_note-auto-44) The Franciscans were involved in the torture and trials of Jews, Muslims, and other heretics[[46]](#cite_note-46) throughout the Middle Ages and wrote their own manuals to guide Inquisitors, such as the 14th century *Codex Casanatensis* for use by Inquisitors in Tuscany.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

As well as acting as prosecutors, many friars, particularly those associated with the [Spiritual Franciscans](/wiki/Spiritual_Franciscans "Spiritual Franciscans") and even some [Observants](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor"), were also subject to interrogation and prosecution by the Inquisition at various stages in the 13th and 14th centuries. Notable cases from the Spirituals include [Angelo da Clareno](/wiki/Angelo_da_Clareno "Angelo da Clareno") and [Bernard Délicieux](/wiki/Bernard_D%C3%A9licieux "Bernard Délicieux"). Notable examples of Observants include the four burned during the suppression of the Observant houses in 1318 mentioned above.

Some 300,000 Jews, up to a quarter of the Spanish population, had to convert to Catholicism or flee Spain, or were killed in the [Spanish Inquisition](/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Inquisition spread to the new world during the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") to root out heretics, leading further persecution and execution (e.g., [Mexican Inquisition](/wiki/Mexican_Inquisition "Mexican Inquisition")).[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### New World missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: New World missions")]

Main articles: [Spanish missions in California](/wiki/Spanish_missions_in_California "Spanish missions in California"), [Spanish missions in New Mexico](/wiki/Spanish_missions_in_New_Mexico "Spanish missions in New Mexico"), [Junipero Serra](/wiki/Junipero_Serra "Junipero Serra"), [Franciscan missions to the Maya](/wiki/Franciscan_missions_to_the_Maya "Franciscan missions to the Maya"), [Franciscan Missions in the Sierra Gorda](/wiki/Franciscan_Missions_in_the_Sierra_Gorda "Franciscan Missions in the Sierra Gorda"), and [Mexican secularization act of 1833](/wiki/Mexican_secularization_act_of_1833 "Mexican secularization act of 1833")

The work of the Franciscans in New Spain began in 1523, when three Flemish friars—Juan de Ayora, Pedro de Tecto, and Pedro de Gante—reached the central highlands. Their impact as missionaries was limited at first, since two of them died on [Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés")'s expedition to Central America in 1524, but Fray Pedro de Gante initiated the evangelization process and studied the [Nahuatl language](/wiki/Nahuatl_language "Nahuatl language") through his contacts with children of the Indian elite from the city of [Tetzcoco](/wiki/Tetzcoco_(altepetl) "Tetzcoco (altepetl)").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In May 1524, the [Twelve Apostles of Mexico](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles_of_Mexico "Twelve Apostles of Mexico") arrived, led by [Martín de Valencia](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_de_Valencia "Martín de Valencia"). There they built the [Convento Grande de San Francisco](/wiki/Convent_of_San_Francisco,_Madero_Street,_Mexico_City "Convent of San Francisco, Madero Street, Mexico City"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) which became Franciscan headquarters for [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") for the next three hundred years.

### First Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: First Order")]

#### Order of Friars Minor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Order of Friars Minor")]

The **[Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor")** (**OFM**) has 1,500 houses in about 100 provinces and *custodiae*, with about 16,000 members. In 1897, [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII") combined the Observants, [Discalced](/wiki/Discalced "Discalced") (Alcantarines), Recollects, and Riformati into one order under general constitutions. While the Capuchins and Conventuals wanted the reunited Observants to be referred to as The Order of Friars Minor of the Leonine Union, they were instead called simply the [Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor"). Despite the tensions caused by this forced union, the Order grew from 1897 to reach a peak of 26,000 members in the 1960s before declining after the 1970s. The Order is headed by a Minister General, who since July 2021 is Father Massimo Fusarelli.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

#### Order of Friars Minor Conventual

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Order of Friars Minor Conventual")]

The **[Order of Friars Minor Conventual](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual")** (**OFM Conv.**) consists of 290 houses worldwide, with a total of almost 5,000 friars. They have experienced growth in this century throughout the world. They are located in Italy, the United States, Canada, Australia, and throughout Latin America, and Africa. They are the largest in number in Poland because of the work and inspiration of [Maximilian Kolbe](/wiki/Maximilian_Kolbe "Maximilian Kolbe").

#### Order of Friars Minor Capuchin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Order of Friars Minor Capuchin")]

The **[Order of Friars Minor Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin")** (**OFM Cap.**) are the youngest branch of Franciscans, founded in 1525 by Matteo Serafini ([Matteo Bassi](/wiki/Matteo_Bassi "Matteo Bassi"), Matteo da Bascio), an Observant friar, who felt himself called to an even stricter observance of Franciscan austerity. With the support of the Papal Court, the new branch received early recognition and grew fast, first in Italy and after 1574 all over Europe and throughout the world. The Capuchins eventually became a separate order in 1619.

The name Capuchins refers to the particular shape of the long hood or *capuce*. Originally a popular nickname, it has become a part of the official name of the order. The order now exists in 106 countries all over the world, with around 10,500 brothers living in more than 1,700 communities, known as fraternities, or friaries.

### Second Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Second Order")]

#### Poor Clares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Poor Clares")]

The **[Poor Clares](/wiki/Poor_Clares "Poor Clares")**, officially the **Order of Saint Clare**, are members of a [contemplative order](/wiki/Enclosed_religious_orders "Enclosed religious orders") of [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun") in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). The Poor Clares were the second [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") order to be established. Founded by [Clare of Assisi](/wiki/Clare_of_Assisi "Clare of Assisi") and [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") on [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") in 1212, they were organized after the [Order of Friars Minor](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") (the first order), and before the Third Order of Saint Francis. In 2011, there were over 20,000 Poor Clare nuns in over 75 countries throughout the world. They follow several different observances and are organized into federations.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

The Poor Clares follow the *[Rule of St. Clare](/wiki/Rule_of_St._Clare "Rule of St. Clare")* which was approved by Pope [Innocent IV](/wiki/Innocent_IV "Innocent IV") the day before Clare's death in 1253. The main branch of the Order (**OSC**) follows the observance of Pope Urban. Other branches established since that time, who operate under their own unique [constitutions](/wiki/Constitutions "Constitutions"), are the [Colettine Poor Clares](/wiki/Colettine_Poor_Clares "Colettine Poor Clares") (**PCC** – founded 1410), the [Capuchin Poor Clares](/wiki/Capuchin_Poor_Clares "Capuchin Poor Clares") (**OSC Cap.** – founded 1538), and the [Poor Clares of Perpetual Adoration](/wiki/Poor_Clares_of_Perpetual_Adoration "Poor Clares of Perpetual Adoration") (**PCPA** – founded 1854).

### Third Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Third Order")]

Main article: [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis")

The **Third Order of Saint Francis** comprises people who desired to grow in holiness in their daily lives without entering [monastic](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") life. After founding the Friars Minor and seeing a need, Francis created a way of life to which married men and women, as well as the single and the [secular clergy](/wiki/Secular_clergy "Secular clergy"), could belong and live according to the Gospel.

#### Secular Franciscan Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Secular Franciscan Order")]

The **[Secular Franciscan Order](/wiki/Secular_Franciscan_Order "Secular Franciscan Order")**, prior to 1978 also known as the Third Order Secular of Saint Francis, is an order founded by Francis in 1212 for brothers and sisters who do not live in a religious community. Members of the order continue to live secular lives, and gather regularly for fraternal activities. In the United States alone there are 17,000 professed members of the order.

Members of the Order live according to a Rule composed by St Francis in 1221. The Rule was slightly modified through the centuries and was replaced at the turn of the 20th century by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII"), himself a member of the Order. A new and current Rule was approved by [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") in 1978, and the Third Order was renamed the Secular Franciscan Order. It is an international organization with its own [Minister General](/wiki/Minister_General "Minister General") based in Rome.

#### Third Order Regular

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Third Order Regular")]

Within a century of the death of Francis, members of the Third Order began to live in common, in an attempt to follow a more [ascetical](/wiki/Ascetic "Ascetic") way of life. [Angela of Foligno](/wiki/Angela_of_Foligno "Angela of Foligno") (+1309) was foremost among those who achieved great depths in their lives of prayer and service of the poor, while living in community with other women of the Order.

Among the men, the [Third Order Regular of St. Francis of Penance](/wiki/Third_Order_of_St._Francis#Third_Order_Regular_of_St._Francis_of_Penance "Third Order of St. Francis")[[53]](#cite_note-53) was formed in 1447 by a papal decree that united several communities of [hermits](/wiki/Hermit "Hermit"), following the Third Order [Rule](/wiki/Catholic_religious_order "Catholic religious order"), into a single Order, with its own Minister General. Today it is an international community of friars who desire to emphasize the works of mercy and on-going conversion. The community is also known as the Franciscan Friars, **TOR**, and they strive to "rebuild the Church" in areas of high school and college education, parish ministry, church renewal, social justice, campus ministry, hospital chaplaincies, foreign missions, and other ministries in places where the church is needed.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The association of Franciscans (Grey Friars) with education became a stock fictional reference in, for example, the works of [Thackeray](/wiki/William_Makepeace_Thackeray "William Makepeace Thackeray") ("Grey Friars School" in *[Pendennis](/wiki/Pendennis "Pendennis")* and *[The Newcomes](/wiki/The_Newcomes "The Newcomes")*) or of ["Frank Richards"](/wiki/Charles_Hamilton_(writer) "Charles Hamilton (writer)") ([Greyfriars School](/wiki/Greyfriars_School "Greyfriars School") of [Billy Bunter](/wiki/Billy_Bunter "Billy Bunter") fame).

After the formal recognition of the members of religious tertiary communities, the following centuries saw a steady growth of such communities across Europe. Initially, the women's communities took a monastic form of life, either voluntarily or under pressure from [ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") superiors. The great figure of this development was [Hyacintha Mariscotti](/wiki/Hyacintha_Mariscotti "Hyacintha Mariscotti"). As Europe entered the upheavals of the modern age, new communities arose which were able to focus more exclusively on social service, especially during the immediate post-[Napoleonic](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") period which devastated much of Western Europe. An example of this is [Mary Frances Schervier](/wiki/Mary_Frances_Schervier "Mary Frances Schervier") (1819–1876).

##### Third Order Regular in North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Third Order Regular in North America")]

This movement continued in North America as congregations arose from one coast to another, in answer to the needs of the large emigrant communities that were flooding the cities of the United States and Canada.

The Third Order Regular of the [Brothers of the Poor of St. Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Brothers_of_the_Poor_of_St._Francis_of_Assisi "Brothers of the Poor of St. Francis of Assisi"), **CFP**, are an active community, based in the United States, with houses in Belgium, The Netherlands, Germany, and Brazil. These Franciscans strive to live an integrated life through prayer, community, and ministry to the poor, neglected and disadvantaged youth, the powerless, people in need, and the elderly. The Brothers of the Poor live by their vows of poverty (living a simple lifestyle), consecrated chastity (loving all, possessing no one, striving sincerely, for [singleness of heart](/wiki/Singleness_of_heart "Singleness of heart"), a celibate way of loving and being loved), and obedience (to God, to the community, to the church, and to self).

The Brothers of the Poor serve persons with AIDS and people who ask for help, regardless of their religion or their social/economic background. They are teachers, childcare workers, social workers, counselors, pastoral ministers, retreat ministers, religious educators, and school administrators, along with other tasks.

The Regular Tertiaries, officially the Third Order Regular of St. Francis of Penance, who operate the [Franciscan University of Steubenville](/wiki/Franciscan_University_of_Steubenville "Franciscan University of Steubenville"), follow a rule approved by [Pope Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X"). Today this group is present in 17 countries: Italy, Croatia, Spain, France, Germany, Austria, US, India, Sri Lanka, South Africa, Brazil, Paraguay, Mexico, Peru, Sweden, Bangladesh, and the Philippines.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

#### Brothers and Sisters of Penance of St. Francis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Brothers and Sisters of Penance of St. Francis")]

The [Brothers and Sisters of Penance of St. Francis](/wiki/Brothers_and_Sisters_of_Penance_of_St._Francis "Brothers and Sisters of Penance of St. Francis"), is a private confraternity of the Catholic Church, whose members strive to model their lives according to the Rule and Statutes of the Primitive Rule of the Third Order of St. Francis, which was written for lay people in 1221 by Francis of Assisi. Right now there are several hundred members within the United States and a few hundred more throughout the world. The order was started in 1996 by members of the Archdiocese of St. Paul in Minnesota.

#### Other tertiaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Other tertiaries")]

* In 1435, [Francis of Paola](/wiki/Francis_of_Paola "Francis of Paola") founded the "Poor Hermits of Saint Francis of Assisi", later known as the "Hermits of the Order of [Minims](/wiki/Minim_(religious_order) "Minim (religious order)")", and then renamed the "Order of Minims" in 1506 by Pope [Julius II](/wiki/Julius_II "Julius II"). There are mendicant friars, contemplative nuns, and lay tertiaries.
* The Society of the Atonement, also known as [Graymoor Friars](/wiki/Society_of_the_Atonement "Society of the Atonement") and [Graymoor Sisters](/wiki/Society_of_the_Atonement "Society of the Atonement"), started in 1898 as a religious community in the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"). It came into union with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") in 1909.
* The [Franciscan Friars of the Immaculate](/wiki/Franciscan_Friars_of_the_Immaculate "Franciscan Friars of the Immaculate") started in 1970, and became an institute with Pontifical Right in 1998. In that same year, the Franciscan Sisters of the Immaculate became an institute with Pontifical Right. There are also Third Order Franciscan Sisters of the Immaculate, an offshoot of the Franciscan Tertiaries of the Immaculate.

### Other Franciscan organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Other Franciscan organizations")]

* The Community of the [Franciscan Friars of the Renewal](/wiki/Franciscan_Friars_of_the_Renewal "Franciscan Friars of the Renewal") started in 1987, and the Franciscan Sisters of the Renewal in 1988.
* The [Franciscan Missionaries of the Eternal Word](/wiki/Franciscan_Missionaries_of_the_Eternal_Word "Franciscan Missionaries of the Eternal Word") started in 1987, and are now a Public Clerical Association of the Faithful.
* [Franciscans International](/wiki/Franciscans_International "Franciscans International")[[56]](#cite_note-56) is a [Non-governmental organization](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") (NGO) with General Consultative status at the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), uniting the voices of Franciscan brothers and sisters from around the world. It operates under the sponsorship of the Conference of the Franciscan Family (**CFF**) and serves all Franciscans and the global community by bringing grassroots Franciscans to the United Nations forums in New York City and Geneva. It brings the spiritual and ethical values of the Franciscans to the United Nations and international organisations.

### Other Christian traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Franciscans&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Other Christian traditions")]

Main article: [Franciscan spirituality in Protestantism](/wiki/Franciscan_spirituality_in_Protestantism "Franciscan spirituality in Protestantism")

There are [Franciscan orders in Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Franciscan_orders_in_Lutheranism "Franciscan orders in Lutheranism"), including the [Order of Lutheran Franciscans](/wiki/Order_of_Lutheran_Franciscans "Order of Lutheran Franciscans"), the [Evangelical Sisterhood of Mary](/wiki/Evangelical_Sisterhood_of_Mary "Evangelical Sisterhood of Mary"), and the Evangelische Kanaan Franziskus-Bruderschaft (Kanaan Franciscan Brothers). Established in 2006, the Order of St. Francis-Lutheran (OSF-L) operates under the auspices of the [Lutheran Church-International](/wiki/Lutheran_Church-International "Lutheran Church-International"). The OSF-L is based in [St. Catharines](/wiki/St._Catharines "St. Catharines"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In the United States, the Evangelical Society of the Cross Franciscan (the Lutheran Third Order of St. Francis) was founded in 1988 in [Orlando, Florida](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"). It was blessed by Bishop Lavern Franzen of the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

One of the results of the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") in the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") during the 19th century was the re-establishment of religious orders in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), including some of Franciscan inspiration. The principal Anglican communities in the Franciscan tradition are the [Community of St. Francis](/wiki/Community_of_St._Francis "Community of St. Francis") (women, founded 1905) (CSF), the Poor Clares of Reparation (PCR), the [Society of Saint Francis](/wiki/Society_of_Saint_Francis "Society of Saint Francis") (men, founded 1934)(SSF), the [Community of St. Clare](/wiki/Community_of_St._Clare "Community of St. Clare") (women, enclosed) (OSC), and the [Order of St. Francis](/wiki/Order_of_St._Francis "Order of St. Francis") (men, founded in 2003). There is a [Third Order](/wiki/Third_Order "Third Order") known as the Third Order Society of St Francis (TSSF), and the [Lesser Franciscans](/wiki/Lesser_Franciscans "Lesser Franciscans"). There is an order of Sisters of St. Clare in the [Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound") area of [Washington state](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") ([Diocese of Olympia](/wiki/Diocese_of_Olympia "Diocese of Olympia")), the Little Sisters of St. Clare.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

There are some small Franciscan communities within [Continental Reformed Churches](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism") and the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church").[[60]](#cite_note-60)

There are associations of Franciscan inspiration that describe themselves as [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism")—accepting Christians of all denominations, the [Order of Ecumenical Franciscans](/wiki/Order_of_Ecumenical_Franciscans "Order of Ecumenical Franciscans") being an example. The Companions of Francis Apostolic Religious Institute (CFARI), with its roots in independent Catholicism, is a dispersed, egalitarian and ecumenical order of Franciscans based in Pasadena, California, with vowed members in California, Arizona and New Mexico. CFARI members live among the populations they serve and are self supporting in their ministry.
